text:
RESTAGING CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS AND TECHNETIUM-99m BONE SCAN

CLINICAL INDICATION: 70-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), BRCA2 germline mutation. Currently on Xtandi 160 mg daily (approximately 9 months of therapy) with ADT (Lupron) maintained. Biochemical concern for disease progression: two consecutive rising PSA values over the past 4 months, most recent PSA 14.3 ng/mL (nadir on Xtandi was 1.4 ng/mL approximately 6 months ago). Known osseous and lymph node metastatic disease. Prior restaging study at approximately 6 months on enza showed partial response in bone and nodes. Referred for restaging to assess current disease extent.

TECHNICAL INFORMATION:
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: Performed with intravenous iodinated contrast (portal venous phase). Oral contrast administered. Images obtained in standard axial planes with coronal and sagittal reformats. Note: patient has mild-to-moderate chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine approximately 1.6 mg/dL); IV contrast administered after nephrology clearance with hydration protocol.
Bone Scan: Technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (MDP) whole-body planar scintigraphy performed at 3 hours post-injection. SPECT imaging additionally acquired over the pelvis and lumbar spine for improved lesion characterization.

COMPARISON: CT abdomen/pelvis and bone scan performed approximately 6 months prior (on enza, showing partial response).

---

FINDINGS:

TECHNETIUM-99m BONE SCAN:

The distribution of radiotracer uptake is heterogeneous and markedly abnormal compared with the prior study performed 6 months ago.

Progression of previously noted osseous disease is identified. The previously stable right femoral neck lesion demonstrates clearly increased radiotracer uptake compared to the prior study. Foci involving the L2 and T9 vertebral bodies persist with uptake that is at least stable, and the T12 vertebral body lesion previously showing partial response now appears more conspicuous with mildly increased intensity.

New foci of increased radiotracer uptake are identified, not present on the prior comparison study:
- Left iliac crest: two discrete foci of abnormal uptake in close proximity, involving the posterior and anterior iliac crest, the more posterior measuring the larger of the two. These lesions are new since the prior examination.
- Right proximal humerus: a new focus of moderate-to-intense increased uptake in the region of the proximal humeral metaphysis/greater tuberosity. This is new since the prior study and represents a new site of disease.
- Sacrum: a new focus of increased uptake involving the right sacral ala / S2 region. This lesion was not present on the prior bone scan.

The left femoral head lesion, previously demonstrating partial response on the study 6 months ago, now shows stable to possibly mildly increased uptake.

The right acetabulum and left iliac wing lesions, previously showing improvement, now appear increased in intensity compared to the most recent prior scan, consistent with renewed activity at previously responding sites.

In total, the current study demonstrates approximately 17 or more discrete foci of abnormal osseous uptake, representing a substantial increase from the 12 lesions documented at the time of CRPC diagnosis, and representing clear progression beyond the partial response seen 6 months ago.

No superscan pattern. Renal uptake is symmetric. Bladder activity is physiologic.

CT CHEST:

Lungs: The lung parenchyma is clear bilaterally without consolidation or pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodules are identified above the threshold of detection on this examination. No endobronchial lesions. The airways are patent.

Mediastinum: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. Aorta is mildly tortuous without aneurysmal dilation.

Bony thorax: Multiple foci of sclerotic change in the thoracic vertebrae, ribs, and sternum consistent with known osseous metastatic disease; findings in the thoracic spine appear grossly stable. Previously noted left 6th and 7th rib lesions are again identified. Right 4th rib lesion with sclerotic change as before. No new dominant lytic rib lesions to suggest impending pathologic fracture.

CT ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilation. Portal vein is patent.

Spleen: Unremarkable in size and attenuation.

Pancreas: Within normal limits without mass or ductal dilation. Given family history of pancreatic cancer (brother), no suspicious pancreatic lesion is identified.

Adrenal glands: Both adrenal glands appear normal. No adrenal masses.

Kidneys: Bilateral kidneys are mildly reduced in cortical thickness bilaterally, consistent with the known chronic kidney disease. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. No urolithiasis.

Retroperitoneal and upper abdominal lymph nodes: There are lymph nodes in the retroperitoneum that warrant attention. A right para-aortic lymph node measures approximately 1.4 cm in short axis, which is new compared to the prior study where no retroperitoneal adenopathy was identified above threshold. An aortocaval lymph node measures approximately 1.1 cm in short axis, also newly visible at this size. Additional retroperitoneal nodes in the right common iliac chain are present and appear enlarged compared to the prior examination.

Bowel: No obstruction, perforation, or mass lesion.

CT PELVIS:

Prostate/Post-treatment pelvic changes: The prostate and seminal vesicle region demonstrates post-treatment fibrotic changes consistent with known history. No new dominant pelvic mass.

Bladder: Unremarkable without wall thickening or intraluminal filling defect.

Pelvic lymph nodes: There is worsening lymphadenopathy compared to the prior study.
- Right obturator lymph node: now measures 2.1 cm in short axis, increased from 0.9 cm on the prior study. This represents clear and substantial interval enlargement.
- Left common iliac lymph node: now measures approximately 1.3 cm in short axis, increased from 0.8 cm on the prior study.
- Additional right external iliac chain nodes are noted to be borderline in size, at 0.9-1.0 cm, and appear slightly more prominent than before.

Pelvic bones: SPECT correlation as above; the right acetabulum, left iliac wing, and sacrum demonstrate osseous abnormalities consistent with known metastatic involvement. The right femoral neck continues to demonstrate sclerotic change. The cortical integrity of the right femoral neck appears grossly maintained, without evidence of a pathologic fracture or impending cortical breach.

Bony structures of the lumbar spine: Sclerotic foci in the L2 and L4-L5 vertebral bodies are again identified, consistent with known metastatic disease. No new high-grade spinal canal stenosis is identified, though degenerative changes are present at multiple levels. No epidural soft tissue mass.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Significant interval worsening of osseous metastatic disease compared to the restaging study performed approximately 6 months ago. Multiple new foci of abnormal radiotracer uptake are identified on bone scan, including new lesions in the left iliac crest (two foci), right proximal humerus, and right sacral ala/S2 region - all new sites not previously involved. The total number of osseous lesions has increased substantially, to approximately 17 discrete foci, up from 12 at the time of CRPC diagnosis. Several previously responding lesions including the right femoral neck and left iliac wing demonstrate renewed activity.

2. Marked progression of pelvic lymphadenopathy. The right obturator lymph node has grown from 0.9 cm to 2.1 cm in short axis, representing clear interval enlargement. The left common iliac lymph node has also enlarged from 0.8 cm to 1.3 cm.

3. New retroperitoneal adenopathy. Previously below threshold, a right para-aortic lymph node now measures 1.4 cm and an aortocaval node measures 1.1 cm - representing new retroperitoneal nodal involvement not present on prior examination.

4. No hepatic lesions identified on this examination. No pulmonary metastases. No visceral metastatic disease at this time.

5. Mild chronic renal cortical thinning, consistent with known chronic kidney disease; no new obstructive uropathy.

6. Cortical integrity of the right femoral neck appears grossly maintained on CT; no fracture identified, though this region warrants clinical correlation given symptomatology and orthopaedic assessment may be appropriate.

Overall, these findings are consistent with widespread progression of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer involving bone and regional/retroperitoneal lymph nodes, in the setting of rising PSA, following an initial period of response to Xtandi.

Reporting Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging / Nuclear Medicine]
Transcribed and reviewed.
